figures.gloss: The absolute, eternal, and ineffable source of all light and goodness, dwelling in the World of Light. It is completely removed from the creation of the flawed material universe, existing as a boundless, impersonal, and living spiritual force.
figures.facet: It is strictly an 'alien' God that did not will the physical universe into being. By entirely divorcing the supreme God from creation, Mandaeism absolves the Great Life of any responsibility for material suffering or cosmic evil.
figures.provenance: The Ginza Rabba (The Great Treasure); Mandaean canonical prayers (Qolasta).

figures.gloss: The principal savior-spirit (uthra) sent from the World of Light. He descends into the dark, material realm to instruct Adam and humanity, bind the forces of the underworld, and impart the saving gnosis necessary for the soul's ascent.
figures.facet: He provides salvation purely through liberating knowledge and ritual practice, not through vicarious suffering or blood atonement. He is a conqueror of dark archons, wielding divine light as a weapon against ignorance.
figures.provenance: The Ginza Rabba; Mandaean Book of John (Drasia d-Iahia).

figures.gloss: The flawed architect of the material universe (Tibil). Through a mixture of divine decree and hubris, Ptahil fashions the earth and the physical human body, but he is completely powerless to animate the flesh with a living soul.
figures.facet: He demotes the orthodox Abrahamic 'Creator God' to a bungling, lower-tier artisan. He weaves a fleshly prison for the divine spark, proving that the material world is a botched, inherently corrupted project rather than a 'good' creation.

figures.gloss: The queen of the World of Darkness and mother of demons. She rules the planetary spheres and zodiac signs, which she uses as mechanisms of fate to trap human souls in the material realm and prevent their ascension.
figures.facet: She is a radical, deliberate inversion of the Abrahamic 'Holy Spirit' (bearing the exact same name in Aramaic). She transforms the orthodox life-giving breath into a malevolent, seductive arch-demon of lust and material entanglement.

figures.gloss: The terrifying, giant dragon of the World of Darkness. As the son and consort of Ruha, he encircles the material world, threatening to consume the souls of the dead as they attempt their perilous ascent to the World of Light.
figures.facet: He represents the crushing, inescapable weight of the material abyss. Rather than a beast to be slain at the end of time, he is a structural component of the universe, forming the very barrier souls must navigate past.

figures.gloss: The greatest and final earthly prophet of Mandaeism. He did not found the religion, but restored and perfected its ancient rites, preaching a gospel of ongoing purification through immersion in the flowing waters of the Jordan.
figures.facet: He is elevated far above Jesus, whom Mandaeans view as a former disciple of Yuhana who betrayed the mysteries. Yuhana champions continuous, repeated baptism for lifelong spiritual healing, rejecting the idea of a single, unrepeatable initiation.
figures.provenance: The Mandaean Book of John (Drasia d-Iahia); Haran Gawaita.

figures.gloss: The central sacrament of full bodily immersion in 'yardena' (flowing, living water). It is performed repeatedly on Sundays and special occasions to wash away sins, heal the body, and re-establish the soul's link to the World of Light.
figures.facet: The flowing river is not merely a symbolic cleanser, but a literal, physical extension of the World of Light flowing into the World of Darkness. Entering the water is a direct, somatic step into the divine realm.
figures.provenance: Qolasta (canonical prayer book); unbroken oral and ritual tradition.

figures.gloss: The ancient, massive uthra who sits at the outermost boundary of the World of Light. He guards the final threshold, weighing the ascending souls (the mana) on his great scales to determine their purity and readiness to enter.
figures.facet: Unlike traditional judges who reside in the subterranean realm of the dead, Abatur holds his court at the absolute zenith of the cosmos. His scales measure a soul's gnosis and detachment from matter, rather than strictly its moral deeds.
figures.provenance: Diwan Abatur (The Scroll of Abatur); Ginza Rabba.

figures.gloss: The principal Mandaean scripture, compiled from originally independent components: the Right Ginza (cosmology, theology, and polemic against rival faiths) and the Left Ginza (hymns for the ascent of the soul after death).
figures.facet: It is the load-bearing text the whole roster's cosmology already presupposes — the source, not a later commentary on it.
figures.provenance: Mark Lidzbarski, Ginza: Der Schatz oder das grosse Buch der Mandäer, German edition and translation (1925); the Ginza Rabba manuscript tradition itself.

figures.gloss: Radiant uthra who arms himself and descends through the successive worlds of darkness in the great pre-creation harrowing, subduing their kings — chiefly Ruha and the dragon Ur — seizing their secrets, and in some texts baptizing Adam as the first man.
figures.facet: Where Manda d-Hayyi saves through instruction, Hibil Ziwa saves through conquest; his harrowing precedes and enables creation itself, making him the tradition's true chaoskampf figure.
figures.provenance: The Right Ginza, Book 5 (the Hibil-Ziwa cycle); E. S. Drower, The Haran Gawaita and the Baptism of Hibil-Ziwa (1953).

figures.gloss: The sequence of ascent-rituals and ritual meals performed by priests over the deceased, and in commemorative form for the living, to raise the soul safely past the toll-stations of the matarta to the World of Light.
figures.facet: Where masbuta is repeated cleansing for the living, masiqta is a non-repeatable rite of passage — the ritual system's answer to death rather than to sin.
figures.provenance: E. S. Drower, The Thousand and Twelve Questions (1960); Jorunn J. Buckley, The Mandaeans: Ancient Texts and Modern People (2002).

figures.gloss: The hidden or esoteric Adam: the cosmic soul-prototype of which every earthly human body is an image, distinguished in priestly teaching from Adam Pagria, the outward Adam of flesh.
figures.facet: Every masiqta performed over an individual body re-enacts the raising of this one primal, cosmic soul — the esoteric center of the whole priestly system.
figures.provenance: E. S. Drower, The Secret Adam: A Study of Nasoraean Gnosis (1960).

figures.gloss: The collection of some hundred canonical prayers and hymns recited by priests in a fixed liturgical register during masbuta and masiqta, distinct from vernacular Mandaic.
figures.facet: Distills the ritual utterances into a single scripture, evidence that Mandaean religion is enacted primarily through spoken and sung liturgy rather than dogmatic instruction.
figures.provenance: E. S. Drower, The Canonical Prayerbook of the Mandaeans (1959).

figures.gloss: A post-Islamic-era compilation of loosely ordered chapters concerning Yahya (John the Baptist) — his birth, his baptismal preaching, and polemical exchanges with a false messiah — complementing the cosmological Ginza.
figures.facet: Grounds the cult figure Yuhana in an actual scriptural corpus, showing the rostered prophet is textually sourced, not folklore alone.
figures.provenance: Mark Lidzbarski, Das Johannesbuch der Mandäer, German edition (1915); Charles G. Häberl and James F. McGrath, The Mandaean Book of John, English translation (2020).

figures.gloss: The uthra corresponding to Seth, third son of Adam, whose voluntary death in Adam's stead establishes the measure against which the souls of the dead are weighed as they ascend.
figures.facet: As one of the three uthras (Hibil-Shitil-Anush) invoked together in nearly every Mandaean prayer, Shitil personalizes the standard of a 'complete' human soul.
figures.provenance: The Right Ginza; Qulasta prayers invoking the triad Hibil-Shitil-Anush.

figures.gloss: The uthra corresponding to Enosh, who in Mandaean narrative descends to Jerusalem in the era of the disciples, performs the true miracles and healings there, and publicly exposes 'the false Messiah' before the city's fall.
figures.facet: Completes the helper-triad and does the anti-Christian polemical work directly: in this telling it is Anush, not Jesus, whose deeds in Jerusalem the Gospels are held to misattribute.
figures.provenance: The Right Ginza, Book 2 (Anush's mission); Jorunn J. Buckley, The Great Stem of Souls (2005).

figures.gloss: The toll-houses ranged between the earthly world and the World of Light, each ruled by a warden who interrogates and detains the ascending soul; Abatur's scale stands at the last of them.
figures.facet: Turns the afterlife journey into an administered ascent, a graduated line of checkpoints the soul's gnosis alone can clear.
figures.provenance: Diwan Abatur; E. S. Drower, The Thousand and Twelve Questions.

figures.gloss: A cross-frame draped in undyed silk and myrtle branches, planted beside the baptismal enclosure at every masbuta; Mandaeans explicitly distinguish it from a crucifix, reading it instead as the four corners of the world under the radiance of the World of Light.
figures.facet: The visual seal of Mandaean identity in public space — the standard declaring the ground is under the covenant of light, not the memorial of a slain god.
figures.provenance: E. S. Drower, The Mandaeans of Iraq and Iran (1937); Jorunn J. Buckley, The Mandaeans: Ancient Texts and Modern People.

edges.note: Manda d-Hayyi actively descends into the material and underworld realms to bind and subjugate Ruha and her demonic offspring.
A necessary cosmological dyad: Ptahil can only build the lifeless physical shell, but only Hayyi Rabbi can breathe the living soul into it.
Ruha and Ur are the incestuous mother and son who together govern the structural forces of the World of Darkness.
GOLD SEAM: Both represent an entirely alien, ineffable supreme source completely detached from the flawed material creation.
GOLD SEAM: The boundless, infinite source of light that emanates lesser divine beings but remains incomprehensible in itself.
GOLD SEAM: A supreme creator of unalloyed light and goodness, standing in absolute metaphysical opposition to the forces of darkness.
GOLD SEAM: Mandaean texts explicitly polemicize against Jesus, depicting him as a false messiah who subverted the true, saving baptismal teachings of Manda d-Hayyi.
GOLD SEAM: The ultimate divine messenger who brings liberating esoteric knowledge (gnosis) down to a spiritually ignorant humanity.
GOLD SEAM: The arrogant, blind, or ignorant demiurgic architect who botches the creation of the material world and traps divine sparks within it.
GOLD SEAM: Historically, Mandaeism consciously demotes the orthodox Abrahamic creator to the status of a bungling, lower-tier demiurge, explicitly reversing Genesis.
GOLD SEAM: A feminine divine principle whose descent or fall into the lower realms is directly responsible for the snare of material existence.
GOLD SEAM: The supreme sovereign of the dark, chaotic realm whose sole purpose is to ruin and entrap the creations of Light.
GOLD SEAM: The massive, world-encircling leviathan that physically borders the cosmos and embodies the chaotic abyss.
GOLD SEAM: A primal, draconic representation of chaos that must be subdued by divine champions to allow the proper order to function.
GOLD SEAM: Late Antique Near Eastern prophets of Light who codified complex systems of gnosis, purification, and dualism into distinct, surviving traditions.
GOLD SEAM: The flowing river acts as the direct physical conduit connecting the lower material plane to the heavenly World of Light.
GOLD SEAM: The concept of sacred, living waters originating from a divine, supernal realm that sustain life and spiritual purity on earth.
GOLD SEAM: The precise cosmic mechanism of weighing the souls of the dead against a standard of divine truth to determine their eternal destiny.
GOLD SEAM: The ancient, specialized divine guardian tasked specifically with operating the scales of judgment at the threshold of the afterlife.
primeval sea/chaos monsters embodying cosmic disorder that a divine champion must subdue for the world's order to hold.
the same historical prophet of the Jordan, but Mandaeism inverts the Christian hierarchy, honoring Yahya as the true final prophet and rejecting Jesus.
both are giant primordial monsters bound and contained by higher powers rather than annihilated outright — chaos kept as captive furniture, not defeated once and erased.
The Right Ginza is the primary scriptural source for Manda d-Hayyi's descent, his instruction of Adam, and his binding of the dark forces.
The Book of John is the scriptural corpus underlying the cult of Yuhana; prophet and book are inseparable.
The canonical prayers are recited during every masbuta; liturgy and rite are one instrument.
The Qulasta also supplies the prayers recited over the dead in the masiqta.
The masiqta is performed precisely to carry the soul safely past the matarta watch-stations.
Abatur's scale stands at the last of the matarta stations, the final toll before the World of Light.
Hibil Ziwa subdues Ruha in the great pre-creation harrowing of the worlds of darkness.
Hibil Ziwa binds the dragon Ur and seizes his secrets in the same harrowing.
Hibil Ziwa's primordial harrowing of the dark worlds precedes and enables Manda d-Hayyi's later mission of instruction — conquest, then gnosis.
Two of the three uthras (Hibil, Shitil, Anush) invoked together as a fixed helper-triad in Mandaean prayer.
The remaining two of the Hibil-Shitil-Anush triad, named together in liturgy.
Mandaean narrative attributes Jesus's Jerusalem miracles to Anush Uthra, who exposes him as a false messiah — an inversion parallel to the existing Manda d-Hayyi/Christ polemic.
Every masiqta re-enacts the raising of the cosmic Adam Kasia's soul, of which the deceased's own soul is an image.
Both denote a hidden interior cosmic soul or spark, distinct from the visible earthly body, that is the true self needing to be raised out of matter.
The banner of light is planted beside the baptismal enclosure at every performance of the rite.

A savior figure who descends through the realms of the dead before the world's founding, binding its powers and seizing its secrets — Hibil Ziwa's harrowing of the worlds of darkness against the Christian Harrowing of Hell.
both traditions organize core identity around ritual immersion, though Mandaean masbuta is repeated across a lifetime while Spiritual Baptist baptism is a single rite of entry.

The name itself means 'holy spirit,' but Mandaeism's Ruha d-Qudsha is a demoness-queen of darkness ruling the planets — a deliberate polemical demotion of the Trinity's third person, not a coincidence of shared vocabulary.

dossier.essence: The living religion of the Mandaeans, an ethno-religious community of Eastern-Aramaic speakers rooted in the marshlands of southern Iraq and Khuzestan in Iran, now dispersed largely into a global diaspora. A Gnostic, baptismal faith, it venerates John the Baptist (Yahia Yuhana) as its greatest prophet, rejects Abraham, Moses, and Jesus as false, and centers on repeated immersion in flowing 'living water' (yardna) to keep the soul in contact with the World of Light. Adherents call themselves Mandaiia ('knowers,' from manda, gnosis) and are often identified with the Qur'anic Sabians.
dossier.cosmos: Reality is split between a transcendent World of Light, ruled by the ineffable Great Life (Hayyi Rabbi) and populated by luminous beings called uthras, and a lower World of Darkness. The material world (Tibil) was fashioned by the demiurge Ptahil and is entangled with the dark powers and the planetary and zodiacal rulers led by Ruha; the human being carries a trapped light-soul (nishimta). At death the soul ascends through perilous toll-stations (mattarta) toward the Light, weighed by Abatur, its passage secured by the baptisms and death-mass performed on its behalf.
dossier.practice: A hereditary priesthood of tarmidia (priests) and ganzibria (senior priests) presides; there is no monasticism, and marriage and fertility are religious duties. The defining rite is the masbuta, full immersion baptism in flowing water repeated on Sundays and at festivals, alongside the masiqta (the mass for the soul's ascent), sacramental meals, strict purity rules, and an elaborate liturgical calendar. Ritual purification, not belief alone, is the working mechanism of salvation.
dossier.canon: A large Mandaic corpus: the Ginza Rabba ('Great Treasure'), the Mandaean Book of John (Drasha d-Yahia), the Qulasta (canonical prayerbook), and esoteric scrolls such as the Diwan Abatur and Haran Gawaita, transmitted alongside a continuous living ritual tradition.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds purification as an unending process rather than a one-time conversion — salvation enacted through the repeatable, bodily sacrament of flowing water rather than through creed, atonement, or a single initiation.
sources: E.S. Drower, 'The Mandaeans of Iraq and Iran' (1937)
Jorunn Jacobsen Buckley, 'The Mandaeans: Ancient Texts and Modern People' (2002)
Şinasi Gündüz, 'The Knowledge of Life: The Origins and Early History of the Mandaeans' (1994)
